title: Forensic Chemistry
abstract: AbstractForensic chemistry, the application of chemistry to the law, is used to assist courts in the interpretation of the relevancy of scientific findings obtained from the examination of physical evidence, most of which is organic in nature. Chemical forensic testing, which may involve forensic toxicology, identification of solid drug samples, or trace analysis by means of modern instrumental analysis techniques, is described.In forensic toxicology, samples from a biological source are screened to confirm the presence of alcohol or drugs. Immunoassays combined with chromatographic techniques are most often employed. Solid‐dose drug samples are first subjected to the older spot and microcrystalline tests. Common drugs of abuse are discussed.Trace evidence, a generic term for microscopic physical evidence, may be examined by various microscopic techniques. Photography and high intensity light sources are also important analytic tools.Forensic serology, the characterization of blood and other biological materials, is also presented. Species origin and blood and DNA typing are discussed.
